claim: Developers who perceive AI as a job threat report significantly lower job satisfaction than those who do not
confidence: high — consistent across all 5 multiverse specifications, 3-seed bootstrap stable
source: logs/v3/stage_2_log.md, section Cohen's d and OLS Main Model
caveat: Cross-sectional design; reverse causation cannot be excluded; sample restricted to AI-tool users

claim: Trust in AI output accuracy (AIAcc) does NOT moderate the relationship between AI job threat and job satisfaction
evidence: β_interaction = −0.009, p = 0.856, f² = 0.000002; not significant in any of 5 multiverse specifications (0/5)
confidence: high for null — p is far from conventional threshold (Δp = 0.806 from α=0.05); consistent across all modeling choices
source: logs/v3/stage_2_log.md, section OLS Main Model, Multiverse Analysis
caveat: Null result: cannot distinguish between true null and poor operationalization of moderator (single-item AIAcc). Measurement error in AIThreat (binary, single item) could attenuate interaction even if true moderation exists.

claim: Higher trust in AI accuracy (AIAcc) is independently associated with higher job satisfaction
confidence: high — large sample, consistent direction, survives FDR correction
caveat: Regression coefficient in OLS; cannot interpret causally. Likely reflects underlying work satisfaction confound (happy developers also view their tools favorably).

claim: Work frustration does NOT significantly mediate the AIThreat → JobSat relationship
evidence: Indirect effect a×b = −0.124, 95% BCa CI [−0.289, +0.021]; a-path non-significant: β=0.161, p=0.106; bootstrap n=1000, 3-seed self-consistency: variance=0.000
confidence: moderate — CI is wide (range=0.310), making this a low-precision null; sample N=17,043 provides adequate power, but a-path weakness is the limiting factor
caveat: Frustration operationalized as binary (any vs. none), collapsing multi-select items — likely underestimates true frustration intensity. Cross-sectional mediation; no causal interpretation.

claim: The AIThreat main effect on JobSat is robust to DevType and Country controls (EXPLORATORY)
confidence: high — confounding by DevType and Country does not materially change the effect
caveat: EXPLORATORY — not pre-registered. DevType was self-reported and multi-select; category assignment was heuristic.

claim: Greater work experience (YearsCodePro) is associated with higher job satisfaction
confidence: moderate — statistically robust but effect size is small (β=0.022 per year); WorkExp marginal after FDR (p_adj=0.053)
source: logs/v3/stage_2_log.md, section OLS Main Model and BH-FDR
caveat: EXPLORATORY — YearsCodePro and WorkExp are collinear (VIF≈6.7) and were included as controls, not as focal predictors.
